summary: Gruber recounts his experience at the grand opening of the Steve Jobs Theater at Apple Park, describing the architectural marvel of the building — its column-free glass pavilion, underground theater with perfect acoustics, and exquisitely designed sight lines. He praises Apple's tribute to Steve Jobs, which featured a never-before-seen clip of Jobs speaking at an internal meeting. While awed by the theater's design, Gruber notes the hands-on area was a practical failure — beautiful but far too crowded, failing Jobs's own 'design is how it works' test. He concludes that the theater's debut and Jobs tribute will be remembered long after the products announced become obsolete.
key_points: The Steve Jobs Theater pavilion has a roof supported entirely by glass walls with no columns, creating an effect that seems impossible
Apple's tribute to Jobs was perfectly executed, letting him speak for himself through a previously unrevealed clip from an internal meeting
The hands-on area was architecturally beautiful but practically the worst at any Apple event — too crowded with too few demo units, failing the 'design is how it works' standard
The sight lines from the theater to the main ring building were so perfectly designed that photos look like idealized CGI renderings rather than reality
Gruber argues the lasting memory from this event will be the theater and Jobs tribute, not the products announced
money_quotes: It looks more like an idealized CGI rendering of how the main building is supposed to look, rather than a photograph showing how it really looks.
It's like descending into a large bright atrium that was entirely carved out of stone. It feels built to last, to say the least.
The screen is perfect: it doesn't curve, it doesn't wrap around the entire front of the theater. It's just a big, bright, great fucking screen.
As a hands-on area after a major product introduction, this room fails the 'design is how it works' test.
One of the ways that I believe people express their appreciation to the rest of humanity is to make something wonderful and put it out there.
And hearing Steve Jobs's voice open an Apple event one more time — my god, there wasn't a dry eye in the room.
Five, ten years from now, the Apple Watch Series 3, the iPhone 8, and even the iPhone X are just going to be old products sitting around in drawers.
It somehow feels appropriate, too, that the most interesting thing revealed in the event that came as a genuine surprise, that hadn't been leaked, was a message from Steve Jobs himself.
key_insight: The Steve Jobs Theater is a triumph of architectural ambition and emotional design, though its hands-on area reveals that beauty without function fails Apple's own design philosophy.
